serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Pak Tong Koh is one piece weighing approximately 60 g, as commonly sold at Singapore hawker centres and wet markets.

nutrient_source.title: Pak Tong Koh — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pak Tong Koh (白糖糕). All per-100g values match the input record which carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pak Tong Koh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 76 is estimated by analogy to steamed white rice cake / rice flour-based steamed cake entries (GI range 72–80) reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = 76 × (39.4 × 0.60) / 100 = 76 × 23.64 / 100 ≈ 17.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (22 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.4 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium itself is low at 47 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is low (47 mg/100 g), the high refined sugar load (22 g/100 g) with minimal fibre triggers insulin-mediated sodium retention per AHA 2018 guidelines; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one small piece and avoid frequent consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~76), high sugar (22 g/100 g), high net carbohydrate (39 g/100 g), and negligible fibre (0.4 g/100 g) cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pak Tong Koh has a high glycemic index (~76) and delivers 22 g of free sugar per 100 g with only 0.4 g of fibre; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this food or restrict intake to a very small occasional portion paired with a protein or fat source to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (22 g/100 g) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and hepatic de novo lipogenesis; saturated fat and cholesterol are negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high free sugar content (22 g/100 g) in Pak Tong Koh raises cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation and de novo lipogenesis; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption in line with WHO guidance of less than 10% of total energy from free sugars.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar at 22 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA threshold and promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high refined sugar content (22 g/100 g) promotes systemic inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis flares; individuals with RA should avoid this food as it provides no omega-3, polyphenols, or meaningful fibre to offset the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pak Tong Koh (白糖糕, also romanized as Bak Tong Gou) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese steamed white sugar cake with a characteristically soft, spongy, and slightly chewy texture. It is made from a fermented batter of rice flour, white sugar, and water, which is steamed until set and then cut into rectangular or diamond-shaped pieces. The cake has a mild, clean sweetness with a subtle fermented aroma from the yeast leavening. It is a popular breakfast and snack item sold at Singapore hawker centres, wet markets, and traditional kueh stalls. Nutritionally, Pak Tong Koh is a high-carbohydrate, high-sugar food: per 100 g it provides 167 kcal, 39.4 g carbohydrate, 22 g free sugar, 1.4 g protein, 0.4 g fat, and only 0.4 g dietary fibre. Sodium is low at 47 mg/100 g. The food has a high estimated glycemic index (~76) and a glycemic load of approximately 17.9 per standard 60 g serving, making it clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. The food is not suitable for diabetics due to its high sugar and low fibre profile, and should be consumed only occasionally and in small portions by the general population.

How many calories in Pak Tong Koh?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with exact match for Pak Tong Koh at 0.95 confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 using the closest rice flour steamed cake analogue, which slightly reduces overall certainty for the GI component but not for the macronutrient profile.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Pak Tong Koh are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Pak Tong Koh is a plant-based steamed rice cake containing no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is confirmed zero by formulation. 3. saturated_fat_g of 0.1 g is estimated from the total fat of 0.4 g using standard rice flour fat composition ratios, as the HPB source does not disaggregate fat fractions. 4. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate based on steamed rice flour cake entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for Pak Tong Koh was found in any approved source. 5. FoodOn URI mapped to nearest steamed cake food product class; FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest rice-based steamed cake anchor — neither is an exact match.
